_meta.purpose: Canonical, machine-readable source of truth for every coaching claim in Yalla. The app reads this (tips, the browsable Library, DOI links); EVIDENCE.md is the human-readable companion kept in step with it.

schema.categories: ordered list for the in-app Library; each {id,label,blurb}
schema.evidenceLevels: legend for the `evidence` tag on each study (causal strength)
schema.sourceTiers: legend for the `tier` tag on each study (source quality). We prioritise meta-analyses/reviews in reputable journals; single small cohorts are flagged.
schema.studies: keyed map of source records {authors,year,title,journal,design,n,doi,url,evidence,tier,finding,caveat}. `finding` is the concrete, verified result.
schema.advice: array of in-app advice items {id,study,category,audience,tone,strength,text,detail}. `audience` = ['all'] or goal/context list. `tone` = 'do' (imperative ok) | 'linked' (associational only). `strength` = 'standard' (default) | 'weak' (single small cohort — show sparingly / Library-only).

rules.sourcePriority: Prefer meta-analyses, systematic reviews and umbrella reviews in reputable journals (BJSM, Lancet, JAMA, BMJ, PNAS, Sports Medicine, etc.) over single small cohorts. Tag each study `tier`; flag weak ones.
rules.tone: tone='do' only when the study is causal/causal-leaning. tone='linked' (correlational/marker) must use 'linked to / tends to' and must NOT promise lifespan.
rules.claims: Every figure in `text` must name the OUTCOME (e.g. all-cause mortality, muscle gain — not bare 'risk') and the COMPARISON GROUP (vs the least active / vs lowest intake / vs once-weekly). Do not import popular analogies (e.g. '= N cigarettes/day') unless the cited paper states them.
rules.doi: All DOIs verified to resolve to the cited paper (re-verified 2026-06).

categories.label: Training
categories.blurb: How to actually drive muscle and strength: volume, frequency, progressive overload.

categories.label: Recovery & sleep
categories.blurb: Rest, maintenance on a busy week, the post-session window, sleep, and what soreness does (and doesn't) mean.

categories.label: Nutrition
categories.blurb: Protein, energy balance for gaining or cutting, fibre, hydration, coffee.

categories.label: Move for life
categories.blurb: How everyday movement and fitness track with living longer and healthier.

categories.label: Body signals
categories.blurb: Simple self-tests that mirror your overall health (predictors, not levers).

categories.label: Mind & happiness
categories.blurb: Mood, outlook, relationships, kindness and habits linked to a happier, longer life.

categories.label: Injury care & prevention
categories.blurb: Preventing common niggles and self-managing them if they flare. General guidance, not medical advice — see a clinician for significant or lasting pain.

evidenceLevels.causal: RCT, or meta-analysis of RCTs. Randomisation rules out most confounding — we can say 'do X → get Y'.
evidenceLevels.causal-leaning: Controlled experiments / mechanistic work / guideline synthesis, but not a clean RCT for the exact claim. Direction sound, magnitude less certain.
evidenceLevels.correlational: Observational (cohort / meta-analysis of cohorts). Adjusted, but residual confounding + reverse causation remain. Association only.
evidenceLevels.correlational+mech: Observational for the headline outcome, but acute RCTs / mechanism explain why. Plausibly causal, not proven.
evidenceLevels.marker: Observational, and the measured thing is a proxy for health, not a lever you can train into longevity. Predicts, doesn't promise.

sourceTiers.meta: Meta-analysis, systematic review or umbrella review (strongest; preferred).
sourceTiers.review: Narrative review, clinical guideline or expert consensus over the evidence.
sourceTiers.cohort: Large prospective/retrospective cohort or meta-analysis of cohorts.
sourceTiers.small-cohort: Single small or narrow cohort — weakest; flag and deprioritise.

creatine.authors: Kreider RB, Kalman DS, Antonio J, Ziegenfuss TN, Wildman R, Collins R, Candow DG, Kleiner SM, Almada AL, Lopez HL
creatine.title: International Society of Sports Nutrition position stand: safety and efficacy of creatine supplementation in exercise, sport, and medicine
creatine.finding: Creatine monohydrate is the most effective supplement for raising high-intensity exercise capacity and lean mass during training; daily use (up to 30 g/day for 5 years) is safe and well tolerated. No loading or cycling is needed — ~3–5 g/day maintains stores.
creatine.caveat: Gains come from creatine PLUS resistance training, not creatine alone, and individual response varies.

failureprox.authors: Refalo MC, Helms ER, Trexler ET, Hamilton DL, Fyfe JJ
failureprox.title: Influence of resistance training proximity-to-failure on skeletal muscle hypertrophy: a systematic review with meta-analysis
failureprox.finding: Training to momentary failure gave only a trivial hypertrophy advantage over stopping short (effect size 0.19, 95% CI 0.00–0.37) — sets close to failure grow muscle about as well, with less fatigue.
failureprox.caveat: Judging reps-in-reserve takes practice; pooled studies varied in how close to failure the non-failure sets really were.

restinterval.authors: Schoenfeld BJ, Pope ZK, Benik FM, et al.
restinterval.title: Longer interset rest periods enhance muscle strength and hypertrophy in resistance-trained men
restinterval.finding: Over 8 weeks, resting 3 minutes between sets built more muscle thickness and more bench and squat strength than resting 1 minute, at matched load.
restinterval.caveat: Single study in trained men; very long rests also cost training time.

stretchgrowth.authors: Maeo S, Huang M, Wu Y, et al.
stretchgrowth.title: Greater hamstrings muscle hypertrophy but similar damage protection after training at long versus short muscle lengths
stretchgrowth.finding: Training the hamstrings at long muscle lengths (seated leg curl) grew them several-fold more over 12 weeks than training the same muscle at short lengths (prone leg curl).
stretchgrowth.caveat: Shown for the hamstrings; the size of the long-length advantage varies by muscle and exercise.

caffeineperf.authors: Grgic J, Trexler ET, Lazinica B, Pedisic Z
caffeineperf.title: Effects of caffeine intake on muscle strength and power: a systematic review and meta-analysis
caffeineperf.finding: Caffeine significantly improved maximal muscle strength (clearest for the upper body) and muscle power versus placebo across pooled trials.
caffeineperf.caveat: Lower-body strength responses were less consistent, and habitual users may respond less.

compendium24.authors: Herrmann SD, Willis EA, Ainsworth BE, et al.
compendium24.title: 2024 Adult Compendium of Physical Activities: a third update of the energy costs of human activities
compendium24.design: Reference compendium of measured energy costs
compendium24.n: Pooled energy-expenditure measurements across hundreds of activities
compendium24.finding: The standard reference for how aerobically demanding an activity is: it catalogues the metabolic cost (METs) of hundreds of sports from measured energy expenditure. Yalla reads each sport's MET here to set how much a minute counts toward your weekly cardio.
compendium24.caveat: MET values are population averages at a stated effort; an individual's cost shifts with fitness, technique and intensity.

walkSyn.authors: Magrath WJ, Qiu CS, Hanwright PJ, Tuffaha SH, Khavanin N
walkSyn.title: A systematic review of muscle synergies during a walking gait to define optimal donor–recipient pairings for lower extremity functional reconstruction
walkSyn.finding: Walking is driven by the quadriceps and gluteals in weight acceptance and the calf (gastrocnemius–soleus) in propulsion, with the hamstrings decelerating the swing leg.
walkSyn.caveat: Synthesises lower-limb synergies for reconstructive surgery — an indirect source for everyday walking.

ellipticalEMG.authors: Prosser LA, Stanley CJ, Norman TL, Park HS, Damiano DL
ellipticalEMG.title: Comparison of elliptical training, stationary cycling, treadmill walking and overground walking
ellipticalEMG.finding: Elliptical training produced the greatest quadriceps activity and the greatest quadriceps–hamstring co-activation of the modes compared.
ellipticalEMG.caveat: EMG recorded only one quadriceps and one hamstring muscle; glutes are recruited but were not directly measured.

stairEMG.authors: Lewis CL, Freisinger GM, Pan X, Siston RA, Schmitt LC, Chaudhari AMW
stairEMG.title: Changes in lower extremity peak angles, moments and muscle activations during stair climbing at different speeds
stairEMG.finding: Stair climbing is propelled by the gluteus maximus, with hamstring, quadriceps and calf (gastrocnemius, soleus) activation rising with speed; loading the handrail lowers glute demand.
stairEMG.caveat: Lab study at controlled speeds; a stepmill adds continuous aerobic load.

jumpropeEMG.authors: Lin Y, Lu Z, Cen X, Thirupathi A, Sun D, Gu Y
jumpropeEMG.title: The influence of different rope jumping methods on adolescents' lower limb biomechanics during the ground-contact phase
jumpropeEMG.finding: The two-foot bounce loads the calf group (gastrocnemius) hardest, with the quadriceps as the main secondary; alternating-foot styles shift load toward the thighs.

skatingEMG.authors: Bongiorno G, Biancuzzi H, Dal Mas F, Fasano G, Miceli L
skatingEMG.title: Roller speed skating kinematics and electromyographic analysis: a methodological approach
skatingEMG.finding: Skating push-off is driven by the gluteus maximus and medius (hip extension and abduction) with the vastus lateralis (quad) extending the knee.
skatingEMG.caveat: Small methodological study in roller speed skating, used as the closest proxy for inline and ice skating.

padelDemand.title: Activity profile and physiological demand of padel match play: a systematic review
padelDemand.finding: Padel match play is intermittent and moderately aerobic (mean heart rate ~70–85% of maximum), dominated by repeated lunges, accelerations and changes of direction plus overhead and wall strokes.
padelDemand.caveat: An activity profile, not an EMG study; muscle attribution is inferred from movement demands, and the Compendium has no MET code for padel.

racketDemand.authors: Cádiz Gallardo MP, Pradas de la Fuente F, Moreno-Azze A, Carrasco Páez L
racketDemand.title: Physiological demands of racket sports: a systematic review
racketDemand.finding: Squash is the most intense racket sport (heart rate near maximum), with acyclic 3–6 m sprints, deep lunges and rapid direction changes loading the lower limb and trunk.
racketDemand.caveat: Review of physiological demands; muscle attribution inferred from movement, not a dedicated squash EMG study.

badmintonEMG.authors: Jiang J, Li H, Xiu C
badmintonEMG.title: Characterization of muscle activation and muscle synergism in the 'forward lunge' gait movement of badminton players using surface electromyography sensors
badmintonEMG.finding: The badminton lunge — the defining movement — most recruits the gastrocnemius (calf), biceps femoris (hamstring), gluteus maximus (glute) and quadriceps.
badmintonEMG.caveat: Focuses on the lunge; overhead strokes add shoulder involvement not captured here.

volleyballEMG.authors: Miura K, Tsuda E, Kogawa M, Ishibashi Y
volleyballEMG.title: The effects of ball impact position on shoulder muscle activation during spiking in male volleyball players
volleyballEMG.finding: The volleyball spike strongly activates the deltoids (shoulders); the jump uses glutes, quadriceps and gastrocnemius in a proximal-to-distal sequence.
volleyballEMG.caveat: Primarily a shoulder-EMG study; lower-limb attribution comes from the associated jump-biomechanics literature.

ttEMG.authors: He Y, Fekete G, Sun D, Baker JS, Shao S, Gu Y
ttEMG.title: Lower limb biomechanics during the topspin forehand in table tennis: a systematic review
ttEMG.finding: Despite its upper-limb appearance, the topspin forehand loads the quadriceps (vastii, rectus femoris), gastrocnemius (calf) and hip/trunk (core) through a proximal-to-distal chain.
ttEMG.caveat: Lower-limb focus; energy cost overall is low.

judoEMG.authors: Honorato RC, Franchini E, Lara JPR, et al.
judoEMG.title: Differences in handgrip strength-endurance and muscle activation between young male judo athletes and untrained individuals
judoEMG.finding: Judo athletes show greater forearm/grip muscle activation and strength-endurance, reflecting the sustained gripping (gi) that defines grappling.
judoEMG.caveat: Grip-focused; upper-back and trunk demands inferred from the broader grappling profile.

bjjProfile.authors: Andreato LV, Lara FJD, Andrade A, Branco BHM
bjjProfile.title: Physical and physiological profiles of Brazilian jiu-jitsu athletes: a systematic review
bjjProfile.finding: Grappling is intermittent and mixed aerobic–anaerobic, with high isometric demand on the grip, back and trunk to control and pull an opponent.
bjjProfile.caveat: Profile review; muscle attribution from movement demands rather than a single EMG study.

crossfitMet.authors: Rios M, Becker KM, Cardoso F, et al.
crossfitMet.title: Assessment of cardiorespiratory and metabolic contributions in an extreme intensity CrossFit benchmark workout
crossfitMet.finding: An extreme-intensity CrossFit benchmark reached a peak oxygen uptake of ~47 mL/kg/min (~13 METs) — high-intensity functional training is vigorously aerobic on top of its lifting demands.
crossfitMet.caveat: Single benchmark workout; CrossFit/HIIT intensity varies widely by session.

kbVO2.authors: Farrar RE, Mayhew JL, Koch AJ
kbVO2.title: Oxygen cost of kettlebell swings
kbVO2.finding: Twelve minutes of continuous kettlebell swings elicited ~9.8 METs — a vigorous aerobic stimulus.
kbVO2.caveat: Small sample; continuous swinging, not a mixed kettlebell session.

kbEMG.authors: Lyons BC, Mayo JJ, Tucker WS, Wax B, Hendrix RC
kbEMG.title: Electromyographical comparison of muscle activation patterns across three commonly performed kettlebell exercises
kbEMG.finding: The kettlebell swing produced the greatest erector spinae (back) activation of the exercises tested, with substantial gluteus maximus and hamstring recruitment — a posterior-chain, hip-hinge pattern.
kbEMG.caveat: Three exercises only; also used as the closest activation proxy for the compound lifts in CrossFit/HIIT.

pilatesMet.authors: Vitor GBB, de Oliveira LC, Pessôa RAG, Aguiar AF, de Oliveira RG
pilatesMet.title: Metabolic intensity and energy cost of Pilates exercises: an exploratory systematic review and meta-analysis
pilatesMet.finding: Mat Pilates pools at a light-to-moderate ~3.7 METs — its training value is core conditioning rather than aerobic load.
pilatesMet.caveat: Limited, heterogeneous evidence base, as the authors note.

pilatesEMG.authors: Ko HS, Jung HU, Park TY, Song JK, Wang J, Jung HC
pilatesEMG.title: Comparisons of functional movements and core muscle activity in women according to Pilates proficiency
pilatesEMG.finding: Across mat exercises the external oblique was the most-activated trunk muscle (>50% MVIC in experienced practitioners), alongside rectus abdominis and multifidus — Pilates is core-dominant.

xcSkiEMG.authors: Lindinger SJ, Holmberg HC, Müller E, Rapp W
xcSkiEMG.title: Changes in upper body muscle activity with increasing double poling velocities in elite cross-country skiing
xcSkiEMG.finding: Cross-country double-poling recruits the triceps, pectoralis major and latissimus dorsi/teres major (back), with the trunk (rectus abdominis, obliques) firing first — a genuinely full-body endurance action.

alpineEMG.authors: Kröll J, Wakeling JM, Seifert JG, Müller E
alpineEMG.title: Quadriceps muscle function during recreational alpine skiing
alpineEMG.finding: Recreational alpine skiing loads the quadriceps (vastus lateralis, rectus femoris) heavily, with the outside (loaded) leg dominating; glutes, hamstrings and trunk stabilise.
alpineEMG.caveat: Directly measured the quadriceps; other muscles from supporting literature.

surfEMG.authors: Nessler JA, Ponce-Gonzalez JG, Robles-Rodriguez C, Furr H, Warner M, Newcomer SC
surfEMG.title: Electromyographic analysis of the surf paddling stroke across multiple intensities
surfEMG.finding: Surf paddling — most of surfing time — is driven by the latissimus dorsi (largest intensity-dependent rise), with deltoids (shoulders) and pectorals contributing and the trunk arching to stabilise.
surfEMG.caveat: Paddling stroke only; the brief pop-up and ride are not captured.

kayakEMG.authors: Brown MB, Peters R, Lauder MA
kayakEMG.title: Contribution of trunk rotation and abdominal muscles to sprint kayak performance
kayakEMG.finding: Paddling propulsion is driven by trunk rotation — latissimus dorsi (back) plus rectus abdominis and obliques (core) — with the shoulders working the catch and pull. The same pattern applies to SUP.
kayakEMG.caveat: Sprint kayak; recreational paddling and SUP add balance demand.

danceEMG.authors: Gao X, Jie T, Xu D, Gál J, Fekete G, Liang M, Gu Y
danceEMG.title: Adaptive adjustments in lower limb muscle coordination during single-leg landing tasks in Latin dancers
danceEMG.finding: Dance loads the quadriceps, hamstrings and triceps surae (calves), with the glutes and trunk stabilising landings — a lower-body, balance-driven pattern.
danceEMG.caveat: Latin-dance landing tasks; styles vary in demand.

golfEMG.authors: Watkins RG, Uppal GS, Perry J, Pink M, Dinsay JM
golfEMG.title: Dynamic electromyographic analysis of trunk musculature in professional golfers
golfEMG.finding: The golf swing fires the abdominal obliques and rectus abdominis (core) hardest during acceleration, with erector spinae (back) and gluteus maximus contributing — a rotational, trunk-driven action.
golfEMG.caveat: The aerobic cost of golf comes from walking and carrying clubs, not the swing.

runEMG.title: Averaged EMG profiles in jogging and running at different speeds
runEMG.finding: Running is driven by the quadriceps (vasti, rectus femoris), hamstrings, gluteals and calf (gastrocnemius/soleus); the calf shifts to early stance as speed rises.

cycleEMG.title: EMG normalization to study muscle activation in cycling
cycleEMG.finding: Pedalling power comes mainly from the quadriceps (vastus lateralis, rectus femoris), supported by the gluteus maximus and gastrocnemius/soleus (calves); hamstrings play a secondary role.

swimEMG.authors: Kwok WY, So BCL, Ng SSM
swimEMG.title: Underwater surface electromyography for the evaluation of muscle activity during front crawl swimming: a systematic review
swimEMG.finding: Front-crawl propulsion is led by the latissimus dorsi (back), pectoralis major (chest) and deltoids (shoulders), with the trunk (rectus abdominis, erector spinae) stabilising; the upper limbs supply ~85% of propulsion.
swimEMG.caveat: Front crawl only; underwater EMG is technically demanding.

rowEMG.authors: Gerževič M, Strojnik V, Jarm T
rowEMG.title: Differences in muscle activation between submaximal and maximal 6-minute rowing tests
rowEMG.finding: The rowing stroke runs legs→back→arms: quadriceps and gluteus maximus drive the leg/hip extension, erector spinae and latissimus dorsi (back) the body swing, and biceps the arm finish.
rowEMG.caveat: Ergometer rowing; on-water rowing adds balance demand.

hikeEMG.title: The effects of grade and speed on leg muscle activations during walking
hikeEMG.finding: Walking uphill sharply raises hip and ankle extensor demand vs level ground — gluteus maximus (+345%), medial gastrocnemius (+175%) and soleus (+136%) at 9° — the incline shift to glutes and calves that defines hiking.
hikeEMG.caveat: Bodyweight uphill walking; pack load tested only by extension.

boxEMG.authors: Xu Y, Sun Y, Zhu Q
boxEMG.title: Analysis of the impact force and key technique of backward straight punch in different combat sports
boxEMG.finding: Punch force correlates most with the anterior deltoid (shoulders) and external oblique (core), with erector spinae and lats (back) supporting — boxing is a shoulder-and-trunk action, not just arms.
boxEMG.caveat: Straight-punch technique; one of several combat-sport cohorts.

mmaEMG.authors: McGill SM, Chaimberg JD, Frost DM, Fenwick CMJ
mmaEMG.title: Evidence of a double peak in muscle activation to enhance strike speed and force: an example with elite mixed martial arts fighters
mmaEMG.finding: In elite MMA strikes the core musculature stiffens so the limb (deltoid/back) and lower-limb (quads/glutes) muscles can drive against it — a whole-body striking-plus-grappling pattern.
mmaEMG.caveat: Five elite fighters striking a bag; small sample.

soccerEMG.authors: Brophy RH, Backus SI, Pansy BS, Lyman S, Williams RJ
soccerEMG.title: Lower extremity muscle activation and alignment during the soccer instep and side-foot kicks
soccerEMG.finding: Soccer kicking activates the quadriceps (vastus medialis), gastrocnemius (calf) and hip/hamstring musculature; with sprinting these are the dominant demands of the game.
soccerEMG.caveat: Kicking mechanics; running load inferred from the game's sprint demands.

bballEMG.authors: Fan J, Yang Z, Wang J, Li S, Kim H, Kim S
bballEMG.title: Neuromuscular control strategies in basketball shooting: distance-dependent analysis of muscle synergies
bballEMG.finding: The jump shot is driven by lower-limb extensors — rectus femoris (quads) and gastrocnemius (calf) — plus the anterior deltoid (shoulders), with demand rising with shot distance.
bballEMG.caveat: Shooting action; the game adds repeated sprints and jumps.

tennisEMG.authors: Kibler WB, Chandler TJ, Shapiro R, Conuel M
tennisEMG.title: Muscle activation in coupled scapulohumeral motions in the high performance tennis serve
tennisEMG.finding: The tennis serve runs a legs→trunk→shoulder kinetic chain: leg drive (quads) and trunk/oblique (core) feed the scapular and deltoid muscles (shoulders) that the study directly measured.
tennisEMG.caveat: Shoulder activation measured directly; leg/core are the proximal kinetic-chain links.

climbEMG.authors: Watts PB, Jensen RL, Gannon E, Kobeinia R, Maynard J, Sansom J
climbEMG.title: Forearm EMG during rock climbing differs from EMG during handgrip dynamometry
climbEMG.finding: Climbing forearm/finger-flexor (grip) activation exceeds maximal handgrip — grip is the dominant load — with the lats (back) pulling and the trunk (core) stabilising.
climbEMG.caveat: Forearm flexors mapped to the Biceps token (no forearm token); used for both climbing and bouldering, whose grip/pull demands are equivalent.

yogaEMG.authors: Ni M, Mooney K, Harriell K, Balachandran A, Signorile J
yogaEMG.title: Core muscle function during specific yoga poses
yogaEMG.finding: Across planks, chair, warrior and dog poses the trunk/abdominal muscles (rectus abdominis, external oblique) dominate activation, with the gluteus maximus contributing — yoga is a core-led practice.
yogaEMG.caveat: Selected poses; vigorous styles add more limb load.

palFao.title: Human energy requirements — Physical Activity Level (PAL) classification
palFao.journal: FAO Food and Nutrition Technical Report Series 1, Rome
palFao.finding: Defines Physical Activity Level (PAL = total daily energy expenditure ÷ basal): sedentary or light 1.40–1.69, moderately active or active 1.70–1.99, vigorously active 2.00–2.40 — the standard way to grade a person's habitual lifestyle/occupational activity.
palFao.caveat: PAL is a whole-day average; occupation is one input alongside leisure activity and chores.

paPara.authors: Holtermann A, Krause N, van der Beek AJ, Straker L
paPara.title: The physical activity paradox: six reasons why occupational physical activity (OPA) does not confer the cardiorespiratory fitness benefits of leisure time physical activity
paPara.finding: Occupational physical activity does not deliver the cardiorespiratory-fitness and heart-health benefits of leisure-time exercise: it tends to be low-intensity but long-duration with little recovery, keeping 24-hour heart rate and blood pressure elevated. A physical job is not a substitute for training.
paPara.caveat: A framework paper; the size of the effect varies by job and individual.

opaMort.authors: Coenen P, Huysmans MA, Holtermann A, et al.
opaMort.title: Do highly physically active workers die early? A systematic review with meta-analysis of data from 193,696 participants
opaMort.finding: Men with high occupational physical activity had an 18% higher risk of early death than those with low occupational activity — the opposite of the protective effect seen for leisure-time activity.
opaMort.caveat: Observational; significant for men, not for women, with possible residual confounding.

rosenblat19.authors: Rosenblat MA, Perrotta AS, Vicenzino B
rosenblat19.title: Polarized vs. threshold training intensity distribution on endurance sport performance: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
rosenblat19.finding: In trained endurance athletes, a polarized distribution (most training easy, a small share very hard) produced greater improvements in endurance performance than a threshold-focused 'moderate middle' approach.
rosenblat19.caveat: Trials were heterogeneous and in already-trained athletes; the best split for beginners is less clear.

wilson12.authors: Wilson JM, Marin PJ, Rhea MR, Wilson SMC, Loenneke JP, Anderson JC
wilson12.title: Concurrent training: a meta-analysis examining interference of aerobic and resistance exercises
wilson12.finding: Adding aerobic work to resistance training attenuated hypertrophy (effect size 0.85 concurrent vs 1.23 strength-only) and power; interference scaled with the frequency and duration of the aerobic work, and running interfered more than cycling.
wilson12.caveat: Effect sizes rather than direct size measures; modest aerobic doses showed little interference.

milanovic15.authors: Milanović Z, Sporiš G, Weston M
milanovic15.title: Effectiveness of high-intensity interval training (HIT) and continuous endurance training for VO2max improvements: a systematic review and meta-analysis of controlled trials
milanovic15.design: Systematic review & meta-analysis of controlled trials
milanovic15.finding: Both interval and continuous endurance training raised VO2max in healthy adults; interval training delivered comparable or slightly greater gains time-efficiently, with larger improvements in those who started less fit.

arem15.authors: Arem H, Moore SC, Patel A, et al.
arem15.title: Leisure time physical activity and mortality: a detailed pooled analysis of the dose-response relationship
arem15.finding: Meeting the ~150 min/week moderate-activity recommendation was linked to ~31% lower all-cause mortality vs no leisure-time activity (HR 0.69); the benefit kept rising to ~3–5× the minimum (~39% lower) then plateaued, with no harm at high volumes.
arem15.caveat: Observational (self-reported activity); associations, not proof of cause.

sch17.authors: Schoenfeld BJ, Ogborn D, Krieger JW
sch17.title: Dose-response relationship between weekly resistance training volume and increases in muscle mass: a systematic review and meta-analysis
sch17.finding: Graded dose–response: each added weekly set ≈ +0.37% muscle gain, with growth still rising across the studied range. ~10 sets/muscle/week is a common practical target (where most of the benefit has accrued), not a hard plateau the paper identifies.
sch17.caveat: Per-set gains diminish at higher volumes; the meta-analysis did not find a clear ceiling.

drr.authors: Pelland JC, Remmert JF, Robinson ZP, Hinson SR, Zourdos MC
drr.title: The resistance training dose response: meta-regressions exploring the effects of weekly volume and frequency on muscle hypertrophy and strength gains
drr.note: Counting indirect (secondary) muscle sets as 0.5 best explained hypertrophy, which is how this app weighs assistance work.
drr.finding: More weekly sets → more growth across the studied range with diminishing returns; counting indirect/secondary sets as ~0.5 best fit the hypertrophy data; frequency has little effect once weekly volume is equated.
drr.caveat: Diminishing returns per added set, especially for strength.

sch16.authors: Schoenfeld BJ, Ogborn D, Krieger JW
sch16.title: Effects of resistance training frequency on measures of muscle hypertrophy: a systematic review and meta-analysis
sch16.finding: At matched weekly volume, training a muscle ~2×/week modestly out-grew once weekly.

sch10.title: The mechanisms of muscle hypertrophy and their application to resistance training
sch10.note: Progressive tension overload is a primary driver of growth — why “growing” needs a rising trend, not just an adequate dose held flat.
sch10.finding: Mechanical tension from progressively heavier/effortful loading is a primary hypertrophy driver (metabolic stress and muscle damage secondary).

plotkin.authors: Plotkin D, Coleman M, Van Every D, et al.
plotkin.title: Progressive overload without progressing load? The effects of load or repetition progression on muscular adaptations
plotkin.note: Overload via added reps or added load drove similar growth — why the trend tracks total volume (load×reps×sets), not load alone.
plotkin.finding: Progressing reps and progressing load produced near-identical hypertrophy and strength.

rpvol.title: Training volume landmarks for muscle growth (MEV/MAV/MRV)
rpvol.note: Basis for the ~10-set weekly target ring.
rpvol.finding: A productive weekly-volume window exists (~MEV 10 → MAV 20 sets/muscle); too little under-stimulates, too much adds fatigue; planned deloads clear accumulated fatigue.

mps.authors: Phillips SM, Tipton KD, Aarsland A, Wolf SE, Wolfe RR
mps.title: Mixed muscle protein synthesis and breakdown after resistance exercise in humans
mps.design: Experimental, tracer-measured FSR at rest/3/24/48 h
mps.note: Muscle-building signalling is elevated ~24–48 h per session, the basis for tracking volume by the week.
mps.finding: Muscle protein synthesis rose sharply after a session — roughly doubling (~+100%) by 24 h — and stayed elevated for about 24–48 h.
mps.caveat: Untrained subjects; window is shorter in trained lifters.

bickel.authors: Bickel CS, Cross JM, Bamman MM
bickel.title: Exercise dosing to retain resistance-training adaptations in young and older adults
bickel.design: Controlled trial: 16-wk build, then 32-wk reduced dose
bickel.note: After a build phase, muscle size was maintained on as little as ~1/3 (older) to ~1/9 (young) of the volume that built it — the basis for the maintenance-volume floor below which a muscle reads as “holding”, not growing.
bickel.finding: After building, size/strength were maintained on as little as ~1/3 of the volume in older adults and ~1/9 in young adults (down to ~1 set, 1×/week) over 32 weeks.

mujika.title: Detraining: loss of training-induced physiological and performance adaptations. Part I — short-term insufficient training stimulus
mujika.note: With too little stimulus, muscle fibres atrophy and strength declines within weeks — the basis for the “shrinking” state when weekly volume drops below maintenance.
mujika.finding: Strength is largely retained for ~2–4 weeks without training; longer cessation brings measurable fibre atrophy and aerobic decline.

vigotsky.authors: Vigotsky AD, Halperin I, Trajano GS, Vieira TM
vigotsky.title: Longing for a longitudinal proxy: acutely measured surface EMG amplitude is not a validated predictor of muscle hypertrophy
vigotsky.note: Why this app weighs whole sets, not per-exercise EMG “activation”.
vigotsky.finding: Acute surface-EMG 'activation' is not a validated predictor of long-term muscle growth.

morton.authors: Morton RW, Murphy KT, McKellar SR, et al.
morton.title: A systematic review, meta-analysis and meta-regression of the effect of protein supplementation on resistance training-induced gains in muscle mass and strength in healthy adults
morton.finding: Protein supplementation added ~+0.3 kg lean mass plus strength; benefit plateaued at about 1.6 g/kg/day.

slater.authors: Slater GJ, Dieter BP, Marsh DJ, Helms ER, Shaw G, Iraki J
slater.title: Is an energy surplus required to maximize skeletal muscle hypertrophy associated with resistance training?
slater.finding: A modest energy surplus supports muscle gain; large surpluses add mostly fat, especially in trained lifters.

garthe.authors: Garthe I, Raastad T, Refsnes PE, Sundgot-Borgen J
garthe.title: Effect of nutritional intervention on body composition and performance in elite athletes
garthe.finding: Faster weight gain (+3.9% vs +1.5% body weight) added more fat without more lean mass — a slower, controlled surplus keeps gains leaner.

helms.authors: Helms ER, Aragon AA, Fitschen PJ
helms.title: Evidence-based recommendations for natural bodybuilding contest preparation: nutrition and supplementation
helms.finding: For lean fat loss: a deficit giving ~0.5–1%/week loss, protein ~2.3–3.1 g/kg lean mass, fat 15–30% of calories, while continuing to lift.

paluch.title: Daily steps and all-cause mortality: meta-analysis of 15 cohorts
paluch.design: Meta-analysis of prospective cohorts, median 7.1 y
paluch.finding: Mortality fell with more steps, plateauing ~6,000–8,000/day at age ≥60 and ~8,000–10,000 under 60; top vs bottom quartile ~40–53% lower (HR 0.47–0.60). The 10,000 figure was a 1960s marketing slogan.

odonovan.authors: O'Donovan G, Lee I-M, Hamer M, Stamatakis E
odonovan.title: 'Weekend warrior' and other activity patterns and mortality
odonovan.finding: 'Weekend warriors' (1–2 sessions/wk meeting weekly totals) had ~30% lower all-cause mortality vs inactive (HR 0.70) — similar to regularly active people (HR 0.65).
odonovan.caveat: Self-reported activity; the unwell exercise less.

stamatakis.title: Wearable-measured vigorous intermittent lifestyle physical activity (VILPA) and mortality
stamatakis.finding: At the median VILPA dose (~4 min/day, ~3 short bouts), all-cause and cancer mortality were ~38–40% lower and CVD mortality ~48–49% lower vs none; ~3 one-minute bouts/day ≈ HR 0.61 all-cause (≈39% lower). (Smaller ~24–34% reductions correspond to a below-median dose.)

momma.title: Muscle-strengthening activities and mortality / non-communicable disease
momma.finding: About 30–60 min/week of muscle-strengthening was linked to 10–17% lower all-cause, CVD, cancer and diabetes mortality vs none — J-shaped, with the largest reduction at ~30–60 min/wk and benefit extending out to ~130–140 min/wk.
momma.caveat: Mortality endpoint observational (the training→strength link itself is RCT-backed).

mandsager.title: Cardiorespiratory fitness and long-term mortality (treadmill testing)
mandsager.finding: Higher cardiorespiratory fitness tracked with lower mortality and showed no upper limit; elite vs low fitness HR 0.20 (low fitness ~5× the risk). Being unfit rivalled or exceeded smoking as a risk factor.

diaz.title: Patterns of sedentary behavior and mortality
diaz.finding: Both greater total sitting and longer uninterrupted bouts (≥60–90 min) independently tracked with higher mortality, dose-dependently; sitting accrued in short bouts (<30 min) fared best.
diaz.caveat: Acute RCTs support the mechanism (movement breaks steady post-meal glucose).

leong.title: Prognostic value of grip strength (PURE study)
leong.finding: Each 5 kg lower grip strength was linked to ~16% higher all-cause and ~17% higher CV mortality — outperforming systolic blood pressure as a predictor.
leong.caveat: Grip is a proxy for whole-body strength/health, not a lever — training grip alone won't add years.

brito.title: Ability to sit and rise from the floor as a predictor of mortality
brito.finding: Lowest sitting-rising scorers (0–3 of 10) had roughly 5–6× the all-cause mortality of top scorers (8–10).
brito.caveat: Single small cohort; a proxy for strength/balance/flexibility, not a lever. Below our meta-analysis-first bar — shown sparingly.

laukkanen.authors: Laukkanen T, Khan H, Zaccardi F, Laukkanen JA
laukkanen.title: Sauna bathing and fatal cardiovascular and all-cause mortality
laukkanen.finding: 4–7 sauna sessions/week vs once: all-cause mortality HR 0.60, fatal CVD HR 0.50, sudden cardiac death HR 0.37.
laukkanen.caveat: Single-sex, single-population (Finnish men). Below our meta-analysis-first bar — shown sparingly.

dmitrieva.title: High-normal serum sodium, biological aging, chronic disease, mortality
dmitrieva.finding: Serum sodium >142 mmol/L (a marker of lower hydration) was linked to ~39% higher chronic-disease risk and ~50% higher odds of being 'biologically older'; >144 mmol/L to ~21% higher premature-mortality risk.
dmitrieva.caveat: Sodium is an indirect hydration proxy; causation unproven.

reynolds.authors: Reynolds A, Mann J, Cummings J, Winter N, Mete E, Te Morenga L
reynolds.title: Carbohydrate quality and human health: systematic reviews & meta-analyses
reynolds.finding: Each +8 g/day of fibre ≈ 7% lower all-cause mortality (RR 0.93); highest vs lowest intake 15–30% lower all-cause/CV mortality. Target ~25–29 g/day. RCTs confirm fibre improves weight, blood pressure and cholesterol.
reynolds.caveat: Mortality link observational; risk-factor improvements are RCT-grade.

poole.authors: Poole R, Kennedy OJ, Roderick P, et al.
poole.title: Coffee consumption and health: umbrella review
poole.finding: 3–4 cups/day vs none was linked to ~17% lower all-cause mortality (RR 0.83), the largest benefit at ~3 cups; overall 'more likely to benefit than harm'.

windred.title: Sleep regularity a stronger predictor of mortality than sleep duration
windred.finding: The most regular sleepers had 20–48% lower all-cause mortality; sleep regularity out-predicted sleep duration.

holtlunstad.authors: Holt-Lunstad J, Smith TB, Layton JB
holtlunstad.title: Social relationships and mortality risk: a meta-analysis
holtlunstad.finding: Stronger social ties were linked to ~50% higher odds of survival (OR 1.50) — an effect comparable to quitting smoking and larger than obesity or inactivity.

lee.title: Optimism and exceptional longevity (2 cohorts)
lee.design: Prospective cohorts (Nurses' Health Study; VA Normative Aging)
lee.finding: The most optimistic had 11–15% longer lifespans and 50–70% greater odds of reaching 85, after adjusting for health behaviours.

alimujiang.title: Life purpose and mortality, US adults >50
alimujiang.finding: Adults with the lowest sense of life purpose had ~2.4× the all-cause mortality of those with the highest (HR 2.43), independent of wealth and health.

bavishi.authors: Bavishi A, Slade MD, Levy BR
bavishi.title: A chapter a day: book reading and longevity
bavishi.finding: Any book reading was linked to ~20% lower mortality (HR 0.80); the heaviest readers (>3.5 h/week) ~23% lower (HR 0.77). Books specifically — not newspapers/magazines. The often-quoted '23-month survival advantage' is the UNADJUSTED figure; after adjustment it is ~4 months.
bavishi.caveat: Observational; book readers differ in many ways.

singh.authors: Singh B, Olds T, Curtis R, et al.
singh.title: Effectiveness of physical activity for depression, anxiety and distress: umbrella review
singh.design: Umbrella review of meta-analyses of RCTs
singh.finding: Physical activity had medium effects vs usual care on depression (−0.43), anxiety (−0.42) and distress (−0.60) — comparable to or greater than therapy/medication; higher intensity helped more.

white.authors: White MP, Alcock I, Grellier J, et al.
white.title: Spending at least 120 minutes a week in nature is associated with good health and wellbeing
white.finding: People getting ≥120 min/week in nature were markedly more likely to report good health and high wellbeing; no benefit below 120 min, and it didn't matter if achieved in one visit or several.

carr.authors: Carr A, Cullen K, Keeney C, et al.
carr.title: Effectiveness of positive psychology interventions: systematic review & meta-analysis
carr.finding: Positive-psychology practices (gratitude, savouring, using strengths, kindness) had small-to-medium effects: wellbeing g=0.39, depression g=−0.39, anxiety g=−0.62, sustained at 3-month follow-up.

curry.authors: Curry OS, Rowland LA, Van Lissa CJ, et al.
curry.title: Happy to help? A systematic review and meta-analysis of the effects of performing acts of kindness on the well-being of the actor
curry.finding: Performing acts of kindness reliably boosted the giver's own wellbeing — a small-to-medium effect (d=0.28), regardless of who the kindness was aimed at.

lauersen.authors: Lauersen JB, Bertelsen DM, Andersen LB
lauersen.title: Effectiveness of exercise interventions to prevent sports injuries: meta-analysis of RCTs
lauersen.finding: Strength training cut overall sports injuries to under a third (RR ~0.32) and overuse injuries roughly in half (RR 0.53); proprioception/balance training also helped; stretching did not.

vandyk.authors: van Dyk N, Behan FP, Whiteley R
vandyk.title: Nordic hamstring exercise halves the rate of hamstring injuries: meta-analysis
vandyk.finding: Programmes including the Nordic hamstring exercise roughly halved hamstring-injury rates (RR 0.49, ~51% reduction).

injAnkle.title: Diagnosis, treatment and prevention of ankle sprains: update of an evidence-based clinical guideline
injAnkle.finding: Functional support plus early controlled weight-bearing beats immobilisation; neuromuscular/balance training roughly halves (~50%) the risk of re-spraining.

injKnee.title: 2018 Consensus statement on exercise therapy and physical interventions to treat patellofemoral pain
injKnee.finding: Combined hip- and knee-focused exercise therapy with load management improves patellofemoral (kneecap) pain and function more than rest or passive care.

injBack.title: Prevention and treatment of low back pain: evidence, challenges, and promising directions
injBack.finding: For non-specific low back pain, guidelines converge on reassurance, staying active and avoiding bed rest; most episodes settle, and exercise helps recovery and prevention.

injShoulder.title: Progressive exercise compared with best-practice advice, with or without corticosteroid injection, for rotator cuff disorders (GRASP): a randomised controlled trial
injShoulder.finding: A single best-practice advice + home-exercise session matched a longer physio-led progressive programme; adding a corticosteroid injection gave no meaningful extra benefit.

injElbow.authors: Coombes BK, Bisset L, Vicenzino B
injElbow.title: Management of lateral elbow tendinopathy: one size does not fit all
injElbow.design: Clinical synthesis; plus Coombes 2013 RCT (JAMA, n=165)
injElbow.finding: Active loading/grip management beats rest for tennis elbow; corticosteroid injection worsens long-term recurrence — 72% recurred by 1 year vs 8% with physiotherapy (Coombes 2013 RCT, JAMA, doi:10.1001/jama.2013.129).

advice.text: If you take one supplement, make it creatine monohydrate — about 3–5 g a day, every day. It's the most proven and safest aid for building strength and muscle, and you don't need to load or cycle it.
advice.detail: Creatine reliably aids strength and lean-mass gains alongside training and is safe long-term (Kreider 2017, ISSN position stand).

advice.text: You don't have to grind every set to failure to grow. Stopping a rep or two short builds muscle about as well and leaves you fresher — save all-out failure for the odd last set.
advice.detail: Training to failure gave only a trivial hypertrophy edge over stopping short of it (Refalo 2023, meta-analysis).

advice.text: Rest about 2–3 minutes between hard sets, not just 60 seconds. The extra recovery lets you hit more quality reps — and that builds more strength and size over time.
advice.detail: 3-minute rests out-built 1-minute rests for strength and muscle thickness over 8 weeks (Schoenfeld 2016, RCT).

advice.text: Favour moves that load a muscle in its stretched position — like a seated over a lying leg curl. Training muscles at long lengths tends to build noticeably more size.
advice.detail: Long-muscle-length training grew the hamstrings several-fold more than short-length training (Maeo 2021, RCT).

advice.text: Before a hard session or a max attempt, a coffee or ~3–6 mg/kg of caffeine 30–60 min ahead reliably lifts strength and power. Keep it early so it doesn't wreck your sleep.
advice.detail: Caffeine improved maximal strength and power vs placebo across trials (Grgic 2018, meta-analysis).

advice.text: For cardio, make easy days truly easy and hard days genuinely hard: a 'polarized' mix — most of it relaxed, a little very hard — improved endurance performance more than grinding at a moderate pace (in trained athletes).
advice.detail: Polarized beat a threshold distribution for endurance performance in trained athletes (Rosenblat 2019, meta-analysis of RCTs).

advice.text: Training for size or strength? Keep cardio moderate and lean toward cycling over long runs — heavy aerobic volume can blunt muscle and power gains, and the interference grows the more often and longer you go.
advice.detail: Concurrent aerobic work lowered hypertrophy (effect size 0.85 vs 1.23 strength-only) and power; interference scaled with frequency/duration; running > cycling (Wilson 2012, meta-analysis).

advice.text: Short on time? Brief, hard intervals are an efficient way to raise your VO₂max — controlled trials show interval and steady cardio both improve it, with intervals especially time-efficient.
advice.detail: Interval and continuous training both improve VO2max; intervals comparable or slightly greater and time-efficient (Milanović 2015, meta-analysis).

advice.text: About 150 minutes a week of moderate cardio is linked to roughly 31% lower risk of dying early compared with no activity — and the benefit keeps climbing to about 3–5× that before it levels off.
advice.detail: Meeting the ~150 min/wk minimum: 31% lower all-cause mortality vs inactive (HR 0.69); plateau ~3–5× minimum (~39%) (Arem 2015, pooled cohorts, 661,137 adults).

advice.text: Aim for roughly 10+ hard sets per muscle each week — that's where growth reliably kicks in.
advice.detail: Graded dose–response, each set ≈ +0.37% gain; ~10 sets/wk is a practical target where most of the benefit has accrued, not a hard ceiling (Schoenfeld 2017, meta-analysis).

advice.text: Under about 10 weekly sets per muscle you're likely leaving growth on the table — but you don't need to live in the gym to clear that bar.
advice.detail: Benefit accrues toward ~10 sets/muscle/wk (Schoenfeld 2017).

advice.text: Hit each muscle about twice a week — the same sets split over two days tend to grow a little more than one big session.
advice.detail: ~2×/wk modestly beat 1×/wk at matched volume (Schoenfeld 2016, meta-analysis).

advice.text: Got a lagging muscle? Add a second short session for it during the week rather than piling every set onto one day.
advice.detail: Higher frequency at equal volume favoured growth (Schoenfeld 2016).

advice.text: Muscle grows when you ask a bit more of it over time — add a rep or a little weight once a set starts to feel easy.
advice.detail: Mechanical tension via progressive overload is a primary growth driver (Schoenfeld 2010).

advice.text: Challenging loads lifted with real effort create the mechanical tension that tells a muscle to grow — that's the core of it.
advice.detail: Tension is the principal hypertrophy mechanism (Schoenfeld 2010).

advice.text: Can't add weight today? Add a rep. Progressing reps builds muscle just as well as adding load.
advice.detail: Rep- vs load-progression gave near-identical growth over 8 wk (Plotkin 2022, RCT, n=43).

advice.text: More weekly sets generally means more growth — but assistance work counts only about half, so train each muscle directly too.
advice.detail: Secondary sets weighted 0.5 best fit hypertrophy data (Pelland 2024, meta-regression).

advice.text: Once you're past ~10 sets for a muscle, spreading them over more days lets you add volume without each session dragging.
advice.detail: Frequency helps accommodate higher volume (Pelland 2024).

advice.text: There's a productive range for volume: too little won't grow, too much just piles on fatigue. About 10–20 sets a week per muscle suits most.
advice.detail: MEV≈10 → MAV≈20 sets/muscle (Renaissance Periodization landmarks).

advice.text: Start a training block at the lower end of your volume and add a set here and there as you adapt — leave yourself room to grow.
advice.detail: Progress MEV→MAV across a block (Renaissance Periodization).

advice.text: When progress stalls and everything feels heavy, take a planned easy week — clearing fatigue lets you push hard again.
advice.detail: Deloads manage the MRV/fatigue ceiling (Renaissance Periodization).

advice.text: Muscle is built in the day or two after training, not during the workout — which is why spreading sessions through the week pays off.
advice.detail: Protein synthesis roughly doubles by ~24 h, elevated ~24–48 h (Phillips 1997).

advice.text: Recovery is part of the program — the growth signal from a session runs about 24–48 hours, so give a muscle time before hammering it again.
advice.detail: MPS elevated ~24–48 h post-session (Phillips 1997).

advice.text: Pressed for time? You can keep the muscle you've built on as little as a third of your usual volume — even one hard set a week helps.
advice.detail: Size held on ~1/3 (older) to ~1/9 (young) of building volume over 32 wk (Bickel 2011).

advice.text: On a hectic week, don't skip entirely — a short, reduced session holds onto what you've built far better than nothing.
advice.detail: Minimal maintenance dose preserves gains (Bickel 2011).

advice.text: A week or two off won't cost you — strength holds for weeks. Rest when you need it; just don't let a layoff stretch into months.
advice.detail: Strength largely retained ~2–4 wk; longer breaks → atrophy (Mujika & Padilla 2000).

advice.text: Missed some sessions to illness or travel? A few won't erase months of work — just pick up where you left off.
advice.detail: Short cessation barely dents strength (Mujika & Padilla 2000).

advice.text: Chasing the "burn" or a big pump doesn't reliably mean more growth — total hard sets over the week is what counts.
advice.detail: Acute EMG/'activation' doesn't predict hypertrophy (Vigotsky 2022).

advice.text: How "activated" a muscle feels isn't a reliable guide to growth — track your weekly sets and whether the weights are climbing instead.
advice.detail: Feel/EMG is not a validated growth proxy (Vigotsky 2022).

advice.text: A consistent sleep schedule may matter more than how long you sleep — in 60,000+ adults the most regular sleepers had 20–48% lower risk of early death than the least regular, and steady bed/wake times tracked with survival better than total hours did.
advice.detail: Most-regular vs least-regular sleepers 20–48% lower all-cause mortality; regularity out-predicted duration (Windred 2024, UK Biobank, n=60,977).

advice.text: Around 1.6 g of protein per kg of bodyweight a day covers most lifters — much beyond that adds little.
advice.detail: Benefit plateaued ~1.6 g/kg/day; +~0.3 kg lean mass with protein (Morton 2018, meta-analysis of RCTs).

advice.text: Past ~1.6 g/kg, extra protein does little for muscle — your training and sleep move the needle more than another shake.
advice.detail: Diminishing returns above ~1.6 g/kg/day (Morton 2018).

advice.text: To build muscle, eat in a slight calorie surplus — trying to grow at maintenance is an uphill battle.
advice.detail: A modest surplus supports hypertrophy (Slater 2019, review).

advice.text: You can't build much muscle in a real calorie deficit — if size is the goal, eat at least at maintenance.
advice.detail: Energy availability constrains growth (Slater 2019).

advice.text: Gain slowly: about 0.25–0.5% of bodyweight a week keeps new gains mostly muscle, not fat.
advice.detail: Faster gain added more fat without more lean mass (Garthe 2013, RCT in elite athletes).

advice.text: Cutting? Keep protein high and keep lifting heavy — that's what tells your body to hold onto muscle while the fat comes off.
advice.detail: Resistance training + high protein preserves lean mass in a deficit (Helms 2014).

advice.text: On a cut, protein needs go up, not down — aim toward the higher end (~2 g/kg) to protect muscle in a deficit.
advice.detail: ~2.3–3.1 g/kg lean mass recommended during a deficit (Helms 2014).

advice.text: Losing fat? Keep it to about 0.5–1% of bodyweight a week — faster tends to drag your strength and muscle down with it.
advice.detail: ~0.5–1%/wk loss recommended to retain muscle (Helms 2014).

advice.text: Crash diets backfire — a moderate deficit you can actually sustain keeps more muscle and strength than slashing calories.
advice.detail: Aggressive deficits cost lean mass and performance (Helms 2014).

advice.text: Everyone chases protein and forgets fibre — yet the highest-fibre eaters have ~15–30% lower risk of early death than the lowest, and most people fall short of the ~25–30 g a day linked to that benefit.
advice.detail: Each +8 g/day ≈ 7% lower all-cause mortality (RR 0.93); highest vs lowest intake 15–30% lower all-cause/CV mortality (Reynolds 2019, Lancet meta-analyses).

advice.text: Good news for coffee drinkers: 3–4 cups a day is linked to about 17% lower risk of early death than drinking none — for most people it's more likely to help than harm.
advice.detail: 3–4 cups/day vs none ≈ 17% lower all-cause mortality (RR 0.83), largest benefit ~3 cups (Poole 2017, BMJ umbrella review). Not for everyone.

advice.text: Staying well-hydrated may help you age more slowly — adults whose blood sodium sat at the high end of normal (a sign of running drier) were ~39% likelier to develop chronic disease and ~50% likelier to look 'biologically older' than their age. It's an association, not proof.
advice.detail: Serum Na >142 mmol/L ≈ +39% chronic-disease risk (HR 1.39), ~50% higher odds 'biologically older' (OR 1.50); >144 ≈ +21% premature mortality (Dmitrieva 2023, ARIC, ~25 y). Sodium is an indirect hydration proxy; causation unproven.

advice.text: The biggest walking payoff comes early: the most active walkers have ~40–50% lower risk of early death than the least active, and the gains flatten out around 7,000–8,000 steps a day — well before the famous 10,000 (a 1960s marketing slogan, not science).
advice.detail: Top vs bottom quartile of daily steps ~40–53% lower all-cause mortality (HR 0.47–0.60); plateau ~6–8k steps (age 60+) / 8–10k (<60) (Paluch 2022, meta-analysis of 15 cohorts).

advice.text: Short on time midweek? People who pack a week's exercise into just 1–2 days have ~30% lower risk of early death than inactive people — about the same benefit as those who spread it across the week.
advice.detail: Weekend warriors ~30% lower all-cause mortality vs inactive (HR 0.70); regularly active HR 0.65 (O'Donovan 2017, n=63,591).

advice.text: Even with no formal workouts, about three 1-minute bursts of huffing and puffing a day — stairs, a hard walk uphill — are linked to roughly 40% lower risk of early death than doing none.
advice.detail: ~3 short bouts/day ≈ HR 0.61 all-cause (≈39% lower); median ~4 min/day VILPA ≈ 38–40% lower all-cause/cancer and ~48% lower CVD mortality vs none (Stamatakis 2022, Nat Med).

advice.text: Just 30–60 minutes of strength training a week is linked to the lowest risk of early death — about 10–17% below people who do none — and piling on much more adds little extra for longevity.
advice.detail: ~30–60 min/wk ≈ 10–17% lower all-cause/CVD/cancer/diabetes mortality vs none; J-shaped, largest reduction at ~30–60 min, benefit out to ~130–140 min/wk (Momma 2022, meta-analysis).

advice.text: There seems to be no "fit enough": across 122,000 people, the fittest had about a fifth the risk of early death of the least fit, with no upper limit — and being unfit was as strong a risk factor as heart disease, diabetes or smoking.
advice.detail: Elite vs low fitness all-cause HR 0.20 (low fitness ~5× risk), no observed upper limit; unfitness comparable to major clinical risk factors (Mandsager 2018, JAMA Netw Open, n=122,007).

advice.text: It's not just how long you sit, but how — sitting in long unbroken stretches (an hour or more) is linked to higher risk of early death than the same hours broken up every half hour, and short breaks keep blood sugar steadier too.
advice.detail: Long unbroken bouts (≥60–90 min) tracked with higher all-cause mortality, dose-dependently; bouts <30 min fared best (Diaz 2017).

advice.text: Enjoy a sauna? In one long study, frequent sessions (4–7×/week) were linked to about half the heart-disease death risk of once a week — heat may stress the heart a little like exercise.
advice.detail: 4–7×/wk vs 1×: all-cause HR 0.60, CVD 0.50 (Laukkanen 2015) — single-sex cohort, weaker evidence.

advice.text: Grip strength tracks with lifespan better than blood pressure does — not because squeezing helps, but because it mirrors your whole-body strength and health.
advice.detail: Each 5 kg lower grip ≈ +16% all-cause, +17% CV mortality (Leong 2015, PURE, n=139,691).

advice.text: A quick self-check: can you lower to the floor and stand back up without using your hands? People who manage it easily in midlife tend to outlive those who can't.
advice.detail: Lowest scorers (0–3/10) ~5–6× the mortality of top scorers (Brito 2012, n=2,002) — single small cohort, weaker evidence.

advice.text: Feeling low or wired? Exercise is one of the most reliable mood-lifters — across hundreds of trials it eased depression and anxiety about as much as therapy or medication.
advice.detail: Physical activity vs usual care: depression −0.43, anxiety −0.42 effect size (Singh 2023, BJSM umbrella review of meta-analyses of RCTs).

advice.text: Aim for about two hours a week in nature — parks, woods or coast. People who hit ~120 minutes report better health and wellbeing; under that, the boost didn't show.
advice.detail: ≥120 min/week in nature linked to good health & high wellbeing; nothing below 120 min (White 2019, Sci Rep, n≈20,000).

advice.text: Doing kind things for others reliably nudges up your own happiness — the giver benefits too, whatever the act.
advice.detail: Acts of kindness → actor's wellbeing, small-to-medium effect d=0.28 (Curry 2018, meta-analysis, 27 studies).

advice.text: Small habits — noting what went well, savouring good moments, using your strengths — modestly but reliably lift wellbeing and ease low mood.
advice.detail: Positive-psychology interventions: wellbeing g=0.39, depression g=−0.39 (Carr 2021, meta-analysis, 347 studies).

advice.text: Friendship is real health behaviour: people with strong social ties have about 50% better odds of surviving follow-up than the socially isolated — an effect on par with quitting smoking, and bigger than obesity or inactivity.
advice.detail: Strong vs weak social ties ≈ +50% odds of survival (OR 1.50); comparable to quitting smoking, exceeds obesity/inactivity (Holt-Lunstad 2010, meta-analysis, 308,849 people). The popular '15 cigarettes a day' figure is a later media gloss, not from this paper.

advice.text: The most optimistic people tend to live 11–15% longer than the least optimistic and have 50–70% higher odds of reaching 85 — and outlook is partly something you can practise and shift.
advice.detail: Most vs least optimistic: 11–15% longer lifespan, 50–70% higher odds of reaching 85, adjusted for health behaviours (Lee 2019, PNAS).

advice.text: Having a clear sense of purpose — a reason to get up in the morning — is linked to living longer: people with the weakest sense of purpose had about 2.4× the death rate of those with the strongest, regardless of wealth or health.
advice.detail: Lowest vs highest life purpose ≈ 2.4× all-cause mortality (HR 2.43) (Alimujiang 2019, n=6,985).

advice.text: Book readers tend to live longer: reading any books was linked to ~20% lower death risk over 12 years than reading none, rising to ~23% for the heaviest readers (over 3.5 h/week, ~30 min a day) — and only books showed it, not newspapers.
advice.detail: Any book reading HR 0.80 (~20% lower mortality); >3.5 h/wk HR 0.77 (~23% lower); adjusted survival advantage ~4 months (the quoted '23 months' is unadjusted) (Bavishi 2016).

advice.text: Want to stay injury-free? Strength training is the best insurance there is — in a review of trials it cut injuries to under a third. (Stretching, despite its reputation, didn't help.)
advice.detail: Strength training RR ~0.32 (injuries <1/3), overuse RR 0.53; stretching no benefit (Lauersen 2014, meta-analysis of 25 RCTs, n=26,610).

advice.text: Prone to tweaky hamstrings? Adding Nordic curls (slow, controlled hamstring lowers) roughly halves the risk of pulling one.
advice.detail: Nordic hamstring exercise RR 0.49 (~51% fewer hamstring injuries) (van Dyk 2019, meta-analysis, 8,459 athletes).

advice.text: Sprained ankle? Modern guidelines say move it early rather than fully resting it — gentle weight-bearing plus balance work, once the sharp pain settles.
advice.detail: Early controlled mobilisation > immobilisation; balance training ~halves re-sprain risk (Vuurberg 2018, BJSM guideline). Not medical advice.

advice.text: Kneecap pain usually responds better to the right exercise than to rest — build hip and quad strength and manage how much you load it.
advice.detail: Combined hip+knee exercise therapy beats rest/passive care (Collins 2018, consensus). Not medical advice.

advice.text: For ordinary low-back pain, staying gently active beats bed rest — most episodes settle, and movement speeds recovery.
advice.detail: Guidelines: reassurance, stay active, avoid bed rest (Foster 2018, Lancet). Not medical advice.

advice.text: Niggly shoulder? Back off the painful overhead work and load it progressively — a corticosteroid shot adds little over good exercise.
advice.detail: Advice + home exercise matched full physio; injection no added benefit (Hopewell 2021, GRASP RCT, n=708). Not medical advice.

advice.text: Tennis/golfer's elbow loves loading, not rest — work it with manageable grip and gradual loading, and skip the steroid shot.
advice.detail: Steroid injection worsened 1-yr recurrence (72% vs 8% physio); load it instead (Coombes 2013 RCT / 2015 synthesis). Not medical advice.
